questions.question: What datasets did 'An Image is Worth 16x16 Words: Transformers for Image Recognition at Scale' use?
questions.gold_answer: ImageNet, ImageNet-21k, CIFAR-100
questions.system_answer: The paper explicitly mentions pre-training on ImageNet-21k and JFT-300M. It also reports results on ImageNet, ImageNet-Real, CIFAR-100, and the VTAB suite of 19 tasks; the additional results section also refers to ImageNet as one of the pre-training datasets.

questions.question: What is the parameter count of the largest variant in 'Training data-efficient image transformers & distillation through attention'?
questions.gold_answer: The largest DeiT variant is DeiT-B★ (DeiT-B with distillation) at 87M parameters.
questions.system_answer: The largest variant listed is DeiT-B★, with 87M parameters. The paper’s reference vision transformer is also described as 86M parameters, but among the variants in the evidence, 87M is the largest.

questions.question: What architectural innovations does 'Swin Transformer: Hierarchical Vision Transformer using Shifted Windows' introduce?
questions.gold_answer: hierarchical structure, shifted window attention
questions.system_answer: The paper introduces a hierarchical vision Transformer that computes self-attention in non-overlapping local windows, then alternates with shifted window partitioning to create cross-window connections. It also uses patch merging to build multi-scale feature maps, and this design yields linear computational complexity with respect to image size. Relative position bias is another architectural choice noted as beneficial.

questions.question: Briefly describe the architecture of 'Emerging Properties in Self-Supervised Vision Transformers'.
questions.gold_answer: DINO is a self-supervised vision transformer training method
questions.system_answer: The paper presents DINO, a simple self-supervised Vision Transformer architecture based on self-distillation without labels: a student network is trained to match a momentum-teacher network across multiple augmented views, with centering and sharpening to prevent collapse. It is designed to work with both ViTs and convnets, but the paper emphasizes that ViTs with small patches benefit especially strongly.

questions.question: Do papers in this corpus agree on top-1 accuracy on ImageNet?
questions.gold_answer: No—reported top-1 ImageNet accuracy in this corpus varies widely from 1.3% to 91.0% across 594 results.
questions.system_answer: No. The corpus shows a very wide spread in reported ImageNet top-1 accuracy, from 1.3 to 91.0 (stddev 8.69), so the papers do not agree on a single value. There are also conflicting SOTA claims: CoCa (finetuned) is listed at 91.0, while other papers report lower SOTA-like values such as PaLI-17B at 90.9, CoCa (frozen) at 90.6, and MaxViT-XL at 88.7/88.51.

questions.question: What is the range of top-1 accuracy values reported on CIFAR-100 in this corpus?
questions.gold_answer: The top-1 accuracy on CIFAR-100 in this corpus ranges from 48.5% to 91.88% across 27 results.
questions.system_answer: Across the corpus, reported CIFAR-100 top-1 accuracy values range from 48.5% to 91.88%, a spread of 43.38 percentage points. The evidence does not list any explicit SOTA claims for this dataset, though the highest reported value is 91.88% (AdaptFormer-Swin).

questions.question: Do papers in this corpus agree on which model holds SOTA on ADE20K mIoU?
questions.gold_answer: No—this corpus does not agree on a single ADE20K mIoU SOTA model, since 7 papers each claim SOTA on the benchmark.
questions.system_answer: No. The corpus shows conflicting SOTA claims on ADE20K mIoU: InternImage-H is reported at 62.9 and is the highest value in the evidence, while ViT-Adapter-L is also claimed as SOTA at 61.5, and SwinV2-G is listed at 59.9. So the papers do not agree on a single SOTA model, although the evidence-based top score is InternImage-H.

questions.question: Do papers in this corpus agree on mIoU values on ADE20K?
questions.gold_answer: No—reported ADE20K mIoU values vary widely across 97 results, from 26.6 to 62.9.
questions.system_answer: No. The reported ADE20K mIoU values vary widely across papers, from 26.6 up to 62.9 (a spread of 36.3 points), so the corpus does not agree on a single value. There are also conflicting SOTA claims: InternImage-H is listed at 62.9, while ViT-Adapter-L is listed at 61.5 and SwinV2-G at 59.9, indicating different papers claim best results with different numbers.

questions.question: Do papers in this corpus agree on whether position embeddings are necessary for vision transformers?
questions.gold_answer: Papers disagree on the necessity of position embeddings; some ablations show minimal impact while others demonstrate clear performance drops without them.
questions.system_answer: No. The corpus is mixed: some papers treat positional embeddings as part of the standard ViT design, e.g. ViT says it “adds position embeddings,” and CPVT says vision transformers “follow the same fashion to imbue the network with positional information.” But several papers argue they are not strictly necessary or can be removed with little harm: CvT says “Removing position embedding of CvT does not degrade performance,” and the self-supervised ViT study says removing them “only slightly hurts performance.” Other papers instead prefer relative or conditional position schemes, suggesting the issue is not whether to use position information at all, but which form works best.

questions.question: How did the maximum reported top-1 accuracy on ImageNet change year over year in this corpus?
questions.system_answer: The maximum reported ImageNet top-1 accuracy rose from 85.2 in 2020 to 90.0 in 2021 and 91.0 in 2022, then declined to 87.8 in 2023 and 83.9 in 2024. So the trend was upward from 2020–2022, followed by a downward shift through 2024.

questions.question: What is the trend in average model parameter count by year in this corpus?
questions.gold_answer: Average model parameter count increased substantially from 2020 to 2023, rising from ~161M in 2020 to ~4099M in 2023, before dropping in 2024 due to small sample size.
questions.system_answer: The average model parameter count rises sharply from 161.1M in 2020 to 291.0M in 2021, then to 780.6M in 2022, and peaks at 4,098.8M in 2023. It then drops steeply to 50.0M in 2024, so the overall trend is up through 2023 followed by a sharp decline in 2024.

questions.question: How did the number of papers using COCO change from year to year in this corpus?
questions.gold_answer: COCO usage peaked in 2021 with 17 papers. It was not consistently increasing: 4 in 2019, 1 in 2020, 17 in 2021, then declined to 10 in 2022, 4 in 2023, and 1 in 2024.
questions.system_answer: The number of papers using COCO fluctuated rather than changing steadily: it started at 4 in 2019, dropped to 1 in 2020, jumped to a peak of 17 in 2021, then declined to 10 in 2022, 4 in 2023, and 1 again in 2024. Overall, the trend from start to end is down (4 to 1), with a sharp mid-period spike in 2021.
